35f62abd-9802-808b-a9b9-fb8ce9e931bd:
# Adaptive Scheduler

*A voice-first reminder app that goes beyond "set a timer" — it reasons about your schedule.*

## What it does

Adaptive Scheduler is a voice-first reminder app built with the **Groq API**. U talk to it, it transcribes, extracts what u mean, and creates a reminder — then fires it and follows up. It doesn't just remind on time; it can prep u before something like an exam, nudge u afterward to check if u actually did it, and answer questions about your past & upcoming schedule in conversation.

## Key Features

- **Pre-alerts** — preps u before something like an exam, not just pinging on time
- **Follow-up nudges** — checks in after a task's duration to see if u actually did it
- **Converse with the agent** — ask *"do i have a gap today"* and it checks real reminders between 7AM–6PM to tell u where u're free, not a guess
- **Voice edit & delete** — just say the reminder's title to edit or delete it
- **Typing box for follow-ups** — reply by text instead of voice when u can't speak, skips the transcribe step
- **Demo mode** — a walkthrough tour of the main features for new users

## Architecture

Built with a router that decides create/update/delete/query using plain keyword matching — no LLM call needed for that part. It then loads one of **4 separate prompts** depending on the intent, so the create prompt only knows about creating, the query prompt only knows about answering. The LLM never touches the database directly; it only says *"call this tool with these arguments"* and a separate layer of plain Python does the real work.

## Challenges Solved

Duration doesn't mean the same thing for every task — an exam has a fixed length, reading a book doesn't, and picking a hackathon problem statement has no sensible duration at all. So reminders get sorted into instant / fixed-duration / self-paced / open-ended buckets, and the agent asks about duration at most once, then moves on with a default instead of pestering.

Also went **stateless on purpose** — the backend resends and rebuilds the full conversation each time instead of holding session state, so it can scale without juggling sessions.

## Why It's Interesting

Most reminder apps just fire a notification and forget about u. This one actually reasons about your schedule — the *"do i have a gap today"* feature is computed from real reminders, not guessed.
38d62abd-9802-8015-9d77-d3168b421580:
# Maze2Race 🏁

*A pathfinding visualizer comparing BFS, Dijkstra, and A\* on a maze board.*

## What it does

Maze2Race demonstrates three different pathfinding algorithms — **BFS**, **Dijkstra**, and **A\*** — solving the same maze board. U can draw your own walls, drag the start and end points anywhere, and watch each algorithm explore node by node in slo-mo, or run all three side by side in compare mode to see the difference in nodes covered and path cost.

## Key Features

- **Three boards** — an open field to draw ur own walls, a generated maze with loops, and an unreachable one to test that the algorithms correctly say "no path found"
- **Slo-mo mode** — watch the algorithm explore node by node, with speed control, pause and stop
- **Compare mode** — run all three algorithms side by side on the same board and see nodes covered + path cost at a glance
- **Draggable start/end** — move them anywhere and re-run instantly
- **Draw Walls toggle** — click or drag on the grid to add/remove walls

## Architecture

Not AI-based — pure algorithm implementations of BFS, Dijkstra, and A*, running directly in the browser.

## Challenges Solved

A* needed to balance "cost so far" with a heuristic guess to the destination instead of blindly trusting either — that's what let it explore way fewer nodes than Dijkstra while still finding the same shortest path.

## Why It's Interesting

i did learn about these when i was learning Data Structures, and A* i learned in my course AI. But to see them in action like this is very cool — watching them work live.
